scenes.text: On January 16, 2026, Mark Carney flew to Beijing, sat across from Xi Jinping, and signed a trade deal.
scenes.search_query: Mark Carney Xi Jinping trade deal 2026

scenes.text: Trump called it a drop-off port for Chinese goods, threatened Canada with 100 percent tariffs, and publicly told Carney — a man he insisted on calling "Governor" — that China would eat Canada alive.
Today, May 13, 2026, Air Force One touched down at Beijing Capital Airport.
scenes.search_query: Air Force One Beijing Capital Airport 2026

scenes.text: Donald Trump descended the stairs.
scenes.search_query: Donald Trump descending Air Force One 2026

scenes.text: Waiting on the tarmac beside him: Elon Musk, Tim Cook, Jensen Huang of Nvidia, Boeing CEO Kelly Ortberg, BlackRock's Larry Fink, Goldman Sachs's David Solomon, and executives from Meta, Mastercard, Visa, Citi, Qualcomm, Micron, and more.
Seventeen American CEOs in total.
The very thing Trump condemned Canada for doing four months ago, he is now doing himself — with a bigger entourage, more cameras, and a state banquet invitation.
Over the next few minutes, I'm going to show you what actually changed between January and May,
why China is sitting at this table with considerably more leverage than it had when Carney showed up,
and what the gap between what Trump said then and what he's doing now tells you about where American power actually stands in 2026.
Before we continue, thanks for tuning in.
Your support keeps this going, and I genuinely appreciate it.
Make sure you subscribe and like the video so more people get to see this.
Let's start with what Carney signed, because most of the coverage at the time missed the structural significance of it.
On January 16, Carney and Xi reached what the Canadian Prime Minister's office described as a preliminary agreement-in-principle.
scenes.search_query: Mark Carney Xi Jinping trade agreement 2026

scenes.text: On the Canadian side: up to 49,000 Chinese electric vehicles per year allowed into the Canadian market at a sharply reduced tariff of 6.1 percent, down from the 100 percent rate Canada had applied since 2024.
scenes.search_query: Chinese electric vehicles Canada tariff reduction 2026

scenes.text: On the Chinese side: tariffs on Canadian canola seed dropped from roughly 85 percent to 15 percent by March 1.
Canadian canola meal, lobsters, crabs, and peas were exempted from Chinese anti-discrimination tariffs through at least the end of 2026.
In total, the deal was projected to unlock nearly 3 billion dollars in export orders for Canadian agricultural producers.
Canada also set a target of a 50 percent increase in exports to China by 2030.
And Carney was accompanied on the trip by the premiers of Saskatchewan and Alberta — conservative premiers from oil and grain country — both of whom called the deal a good day for their provinces.
Trump's immediate response, the same afternoon Carney was still answering questions from reporters in Beijing, was to call it a good thing.
His exact words: "That's what he should be doing. It's a good thing for him to sign a trade deal."
scenes.speaker: Donald Trump

scenes.text: Within eight days, he had reversed that position entirely.
On January 24, Trump posted on Truth Social that if Canada made a deal with China, it would be hit with 100 percent tariffs immediately.
He said China would eat Canada alive and destroy their businesses, their social fabric, and their general way of life.
He called Carney "Governor Carney" — a deliberate reference to his stated position that Canada should become the 51st American state.
Now hold that picture in your mind, because this is the context Trump is walking into Beijing carrying.
The first thing you need to understand is what happened to Trump's trade leverage between January and today.

On February 20, 2026, the Supreme Court ruled 6 to 3 that Trump's sweeping tariffs — the ones imposed under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act, including the Liberation Day reciprocal tariffs — were unconstitutional.
Chief Justice John Roberts wrote the majority opinion, concluding that IEEPA does not authorize the president to impose tariffs and that the power to tax belongs to Congress under the Constitution.
Within hours of the ruling, Trump announced new 10 percent global tariffs under a different statute, Section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974, then raised them to 15 percent the following day.
But Section 122 tariffs are, by design, non-discriminatory — they apply across the board, not country by country.
That eliminates the bilateral pressure mechanism Trump had been using as his primary negotiating instrument.
You cannot threaten China with 145 percent tariffs if the legal authority for doing so has been struck down.
The court's ruling didn't just embarrass the administration.
It fundamentally altered the architecture of American trade leverage going into this summit.

The second thing you need to understand is what China built up in the months since January while the US was dealing with the Iran war, a Supreme Court rebuke, and a tariff framework that kept shifting underneath it.
China controls roughly 90 percent of global rare earth refining.
In April 2025, Beijing imposed export controls on seven heavy rare earth elements.

In October 2025, it expanded those controls to five additional elements and added extraterritorial provisions — meaning that products made outside China using Chinese-origin rare earth materials also required Chinese export licenses.
That last part is significant.
It extended Beijing's reach into supply chains the US had assumed were beyond its jurisdiction.
When Trump and Xi met at the APEC summit in South Korea in October 2025, China suspended the second wave of controls for one year as part of a mutual stand-down.
That suspension runs through November 10, 2026.
The clock is visible from the conference room in Beijing where Trump is sitting today.
The Council on Foreign Relations published an analysis this week noting that Xi successfully beat back Trump's unprecedented tariff escalation last year by wielding rare earths as a break-glass tool.
When Beijing threatened to restrict those flows in April and October 2025, Trump backed down rather than escalate.
The CFR's conclusion: Xi arrives at this summit convinced he is winning.
He has told Communist Party cadres for years that the East is rising and the West is declining.
The events of the last twelve months have given him data to support that framing.

Then there is Iran. The war between the United States, Israel, and Iran that began on February 28 has become the single most consequential variable in this summit, and it is one where China holds cards the US cannot easily match.
China is Iran's largest trade partner and the top buyer of Iranian oil. Beijing has called publicly for a comprehensive ceasefire and negotiations since the first week of the conflict.
Xi's Foreign Minister Wang Yi hosted Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi in Beijing just last week and reiterated that call.
scenes.search_query: Wang Yi Abbas Araghchi Beijing meeting 2026

scenes.text: Trump needs China's cooperation to pressure Tehran toward a diplomatic off-ramp, particularly with the Strait of Hormuz still functionally closed to Western shipping and oil prices elevated above 100 dollars a barrel.
That need hands China leverage in the room. Trump has said publicly he wants to discuss Iran with Xi and that the main focus will be trade.
scenes.search_query: Trump Xi Jinping Iran trade discussion 2026

scenes.text: What he hasn't said publicly is how much he's willing to offer on Taiwan in exchange for Xi's cooperation on Iran — and that question is the one every analyst watching this summit is treating as the live wire.
Now connect that back to what Carney did on January 16 and what the reaction revealed.
Trump's public threat — that making a deal with China would result in 100 percent tariffs on Canadian goods — was, fundamentally, an assertion of American dominance over Canadian foreign policy.
The threat was not about trade economics. It was about hierarchy.
It was a statement that no American ally could negotiate independently with Beijing without Washington's permission.

Carney's response was to describe the deal as entirely consistent with Canada's obligations under the Canada-US-Mexico Agreement and to say publicly that Canada "expects" the United States to respect Canadian sovereignty.
scenes.search_query: Mark Carney Canada US Mexico Agreement 2026

scenes.text: He called it "enormous progress" for Canadian farmers.
scenes.speaker: Mark Carney

scenes.text: He didn't blink.
What happened next is worth noting.
Trump never followed through with the 100 percent tariff threat.
Canada was already subject to a 35 percent tariff on non-CUSMA goods, but the retaliatory escalation Trump promised over the China deal never materialized.
The Supreme Court ruling in February complicated any further tariff action.
And by March, the Iran war had consumed the administration's bandwidth entirely.
Canada's China deal survived.
The canola tariffs dropped on schedule on March 1.
The trade channel between Ottawa and Beijing opened exactly as Carney said it would.

So today, when Trump lands in Beijing with 17 CEOs to negotiate trade access, purchase agreements for soybeans and aircraft, a bilateral board of trade, and potential movement on rare earths, he is doing, at the presidential level with considerably more geopolitical complexity, precisely what he told Canada it could not do.
The specific deliverables being discussed are not trivial.
China purchasing US soybeans and agricultural products — categories Beijing had boycotted during the trade war — is a concrete, verifiable win Trump can present to American farmers.
Boeing CEO Kelly Ortberg is on Air Force One specifically because an aircraft purchase agreement is reportedly being negotiated.
Boeing has faced 125 percent Chinese import tariffs since April 2025 as retaliation for US tariff escalation.
Ortberg has been publicly confident the tensions would not prevent recovery; the fact that he is in Beijing this week suggests there is something real to negotiate.
Jensen Huang of Nvidia joined the delegation at the last minute, boarding Air Force One during a stopover in Alaska.
His presence is significant given that the US has been restricting advanced semiconductor exports to China while simultaneously seeking access to Chinese rare earths.
The Trump administration has already allowed the sale of some less advanced Nvidia AI chips to China at a 25 percent fee.
scenes.search_query: Nvidia AI chips sale to China 2026

scenes.text: Huang's last-minute addition suggests the semiconductor conversation may go further than previously announced.
On the other side of the table, Xi will be pushing on Taiwan.
Beijing wants the US to shift its official formulation on Taiwan from "not supporting independence" to "opposing independence."
That change in language is small by any casual reading.
In the specific grammar of cross-strait deterrence, it is enormous.
Beijing also wants to delay or reduce a 14 billion dollar US arms package to Taiwan that is awaiting final approval.
A senior Taiwanese official told Bloomberg this week that "what we are most afraid of is Taiwan being put on the menu of the talk between Xi Jinping and President Trump."
scenes.speaker: senior Taiwanese official

scenes.text: That statement came from Taipei, not Beijing.
The analysts who have been watching this most closely are not optimistic about a structural breakthrough.
The Soufan Center described the most likely outcome as modest and concentrated trade and technology deals that both sides can claim as easy wins without making major strategic concessions.
Fortune described this as the first of four expected meetings between the two leaders this year, including a likely reciprocal state visit to the US later in 2026.
The framing being offered by serious China watchers is stabilization, not transformation.
That framing matters because it stands in contrast to how this summit is being sold domestically.
The White House has told reporters that the American people can expect the president to deliver more good deals on behalf of the country.
Trump posted on Truth Social ahead of departure that he expected "great things" from the summit.
scenes.speaker: Donald Trump

scenes.text: But inflation in the US surged to 3.8 percent in April, its highest in three years, driven largely by the Iran war's effect on energy prices.
American gas prices have climbed significantly since February 28.
A trade agreement that reduces friction with China is genuinely useful in that environment.

But the structural leverage the US held entering 2026 — massive tariff authority, a dominant negotiating posture — has been partially eroded by the Supreme Court ruling, the ongoing Iran military commitment, and China's demonstrated willingness to weaponize rare earth supply chains.
The photo from the tarmac says something that neither government's communications team would phrase this way.
scenes.search_query: Beijing Capital Airport tarmac press photo

scenes.text: An American president, flanked by the CEOs of Apple, Tesla, Nvidia, Goldman Sachs, BlackRock, and Boeing, arriving in China's capital to negotiate trade access, commodity purchases, and technology agreements — four months after publicly threatening Canada with economic annihilation for doing something smaller, with fewer people, and with less at stake.
scenes.search_query: Donald Trump US delegation Beijing May 2026

scenes.text: Mark Carney signed his deal on January 16 while Trump was calling him Governor and predicting that China would devour Canada whole.
scenes.search_query: Mark Carney China trade deal January 2026

scenes.text: As of today, Canada's canola is flowing into China.
Chinese tariffs on Canadian agricultural products dropped on schedule.
The deal Carney was told would trigger 100 percent retaliation is operational.
And the man who made those threats just landed in Beijing with 17 CEOs and a state banquet ahead of him.
The question is not whether it was hypocritical to go.

Trade with China is real, the economic stakes are enormous, and direct presidential engagement with Xi is arguably necessary given the Iran crisis and the rare earth dependency the US has not yet resolved.
The question is what the gap between what was said and what is being done tells you about how leverage actually works in this moment — and who understood that first.
Carney understood it in January.
He went to Beijing.
He made his deal.
He didn't wait for permission.
Share this if you've been following this story.
The irony here is too large to let disappear into the news cycle.
And if you want to stay updated as the outcomes of this summit come through, subscribe and like the video.
The two days of talks are just beginning, and what comes out of the room will matter for every economy connected to this relationship — which, at this point, is every economy on Earth.
